questions.prompt: Why are evals described as 'the moat' for AI products?
options: They're legally required for AI deployments
Models, prompts, and prices churn — the eval suite is the durable asset that makes every future change cheap and safe
They eliminate the need for human review
They guarantee the product never regresses
questions.explanation: The model you use today will be replaced; the prompt will be rewritten. What compounds is the ability to measure whether any change helped — that's what lets you adopt better models in days while competitors re-vibe-check for months.

questions.prompt: What should come before choosing metrics, per the lesson?
options: Error analysis: read 50–100 real traces, cluster the failure modes, count them
Buying an evaluation platform
Defining a 1–10 quality scale
Hiring a data labeling vendor
questions.explanation: Metrics chosen before looking at data measure what you imagined, not what's failing. Reading real traces and clustering failures tells you what's actually worth measuring — then metrics follow from observed failure modes.

questions.prompt: What's the most valuable output of an eval run?
options: The aggregate score for the status report
The list of failures you then read
The cost of the run
A pass/fail badge for the README
questions.explanation: The aggregate tells you whether things changed; the failure list tells you what to do next. Reading failures is how evals drive development rather than just grading it — vibe checks only ever test the happy paths you remembered.

questions.prompt: Which layers make up a complete evaluation stack? (Select all that apply.)
options: Programmatic checks for everything deterministic
LLM judges for open-ended quality
Sampled human review to calibrate both
A single end-to-end accuracy number that replaces the other layers
questions.explanation: The layers are complementary: rules are fast and consistent, judges cover fuzzy criteria at scale, and humans keep both honest. A single aggregate number localizes nothing and hides compensating failures.

questions.prompt: What is a golden test set?
options: The largest dataset you can scrape
A frozen, versioned set of inputs plus expectations, measured identically across every change
A set of outputs the model produced on a good day
The provider's built-in benchmark
questions.explanation: Golden sets are yardsticks: fixed inputs with expectations (answers, checklists, or labels), versioned in git, so today's 84% and next week's 86% are measured on the same ruler. Frozen doesn't mean static — it grows through controlled additions.

questions.prompt: What's the recommended sourcing priority for golden-set cases?
options: Purely synthetic generation for perfect coverage
Real traffic first — failures especially — plus human-reviewed synthetic cases for edge and adversarial coverage
Public benchmark datasets only
Cases written exclusively by the engineering team
questions.explanation: Real queries capture the distribution you actually face, and production failures are the highest-value cases. Synthetic cases fill coverage gaps (edge cases, adversarial inputs) but need human review — and every new production failure becomes a permanent case.

questions.prompt: Which labeling practices does the lesson recommend? (Select all that apply.)
options: Written labeling guidelines
Measuring inter-labeler agreement
Decomposed binary labels rather than holistic 1–10 scores
Letting each labeler invent their own criteria for flexibility
questions.explanation: Label quality caps eval quality. Guidelines plus agreement measurement catch ambiguity, and binary decomposed criteria ('grounded: yes/no') are far more consistent and actionable than holistic scores. Per-labeler improvisation guarantees noise.

questions.prompt: Why must you guard a golden set against contamination?
options: Contaminated sets are slower to run
If eval cases leak into few-shot examples or training data, scores measure memorization, not capability
Providers charge more for contaminated datasets
Contamination only matters for public benchmarks
questions.explanation: An eval case the system has effectively seen (as a few-shot example, or in fine-tuning data — Module 6) scores artificially well. Keep held-out slices, draw few-shot examples from a separate pool, and treat public benchmarks with the same skepticism.

questions.prompt: What's the economic rule ordering programmatic checks and LLM judges?
options: Judges first, since they're more thorough
Push every deterministically checkable criterion into code; spend the judge only on the residue
Alternate between them per test case
Use whichever the team knows better
questions.explanation: Rules are free, instant, perfectly consistent, and runnable on every commit; judges are none of those. Teams typically find 60–80% of what they care about — formats, presence, schemas, executable behavior — was rule-checkable all along.

questions.prompt: What's the strongest available check for generated code or SQL?
options: An LLM judge rating its elegance
Execution-based checks: does it run, pass tests, return the expected rows?
Regex-matching the syntax
Counting lines of code
questions.explanation: Where output is runnable, run it — correctness by behavior beats correctness by appearance. This is how coding benchmarks like HumanEval score, and a fixture database makes it work for SQL.

questions.prompt: Why decompose 'is this a good reply?' into binary rules where possible?
options: Binary rules are required by CI systems
Each rule failure localizes what broke, while a judge's 6/10 tells you almost nothing — and the rules run for free
Decomposition makes the judge unnecessary forever
Holistic judgments are forbidden by best practice
questions.explanation: 'No citation' or 'over 3 sentences' is actionable; a holistic score isn't. Decomposition converts most of 'good' into cheap, diagnostic checks — and the genuine residue (reasoning soundness, relative helpfulness) is what the judge is for.

questions.prompt: Which are real failure modes of rule-based checks? (Select all that apply.)
options: Over-strict rules failing acceptable outputs (e.g., '$42.00' vs '$42') until the team ignores red builds
Goodharting: outputs satisfying every rule while still being wrong
Rules verifying necessary conditions but rarely sufficient ones
Rules exhibiting position bias like LLM judges
questions.explanation: Brittleness wants aggressive normalization (and promoting chronic false-alarmers to the judge); Goodharting is why rules can't be the whole stack — perfect citations to irrelevant docs pass the regex. Position bias is a judge pathology; determinism is precisely what rules don't suffer from.

questions.prompt: Which judge-prompt design does the lesson recommend?
options: A single 1–10 quality scale for flexibility
Named binary criteria, reasoning before verdict, borderline few-shot anchors, and an escape hatch — at temperature 0 with structured output
No rubric, so the judge can apply its own standards
Verdict first, then explanation, to save tokens
questions.explanation: Binary named criteria are more consistent and actionable than scales; reasoning-before-verdict applies Module 2's CoT ordering; borderline examples calibrate the boundary; and the escape hatch handles malformed outputs. Structured output makes verdicts parse mechanically.

questions.prompt: When is pairwise judging preferable to pointwise?
options: When monitoring one system's absolute quality over time
For decisions that are actually comparisons — prompt A vs B, base vs fine-tuned — because models compare far more reliably than they score absolutely
Whenever the eval set is small
Pairwise is always preferable in every situation
questions.explanation: 'Is this 6 or 7 out of 10?' is noise; 'is A or B more helpful?' is signal. Pairwise fits comparison decisions (with ties allowed); pointwise remains right for absolute monitoring and crisp criteria like groundedness.

questions.prompt: Which biases corrupt LLM judges, with which mitigations? (Select all that apply.)
options: Position bias — run both orderings and count only consistent wins
Verbosity bias — instruct that longer isn't better and check score-length correlation
Self-preference — judge with a different model family than the one being judged
Recency bias — judges only remember the last 100 tokens of an answer
questions.explanation: Position, verbosity, and self-preference biases are systematic, not random — position bias alone can swing pairwise results by double digits, more than most changes you're measuring. The fourth option misdescribes how models process context.

questions.prompt: How do you establish that a judge can be trusted?
options: Use the largest available model and assume quality
Calibrate: human-label 50–100 cases, measure judge-human agreement per criterion, and iterate on the judge prompt where agreement is poor
Check that its scores are stable across runs
Verify the judge never gives failing verdicts
questions.explanation: A judge is a measurement instrument; uncalibrated, it produces confident wrong conclusions. Target agreement near human-human levels, report per-criterion (judges are stronger on groundedness than 'helpfulness'), version the calibration set, and re-validate on judge changes.

questions.prompt: What question must LLM observability answer that traditional monitoring doesn't?
options: Is the service up?
Why did the system say *that*? — requiring enough capture to replay any interaction
How many requests per second are we serving?
Which server handled the request?
questions.explanation: An LLM app can be up, fast, and error-free while giving wrong answers. Debugging needs the rendered prompt, versions, parameters, full response, tokens, and latency — the complete interaction, not a stack trace.

questions.prompt: How does the trace/span model map onto a RAG request?
options: One span total, since RAG is one API call
One trace for the request, with nested spans for rewrite, retrieve, rerank, and generate — each carrying attributes like chunk ids and scores
One trace per retrieved chunk
Spans are only for agents, not RAG
questions.explanation: Multi-step systems need per-step visibility: the span structure is what lets you see at a glance whether retrieval fetched wrong chunks or generation botched good ones — Module 4's localization question, answered in production.

questions.prompt: How should online evals be deployed in production?
options: Run the full judge suite on every single response
Cheap programmatic checks on everything, LLM judges on a sample, alerts on rates rather than single failures
No evals in production — they're an offline activity
Only manual review, since automation can't run in production
questions.explanation: Schema and citation checks are cheap enough for 100% of traffic; judges run on a sampled percentage for cost. Alerting on rate changes catches regressions in hours instead of waiting for user complaints.

questions.prompt: What belongs in a sampled trace-review session? (Select all that apply.)
options: Random traces
Every user thumbs-down
Outliers: slowest, most expensive, most agent iterations
Only traces that triggered exceptions
questions.explanation: Random sampling shows the typical experience, thumbs-downs are labeled failures, and outliers surface systemic issues. Exception-only review misses the defining LLM failure mode: confidently wrong answers that throw no errors. Reviewed failures then feed golden sets, checks, and rubrics.

questions.prompt: Why do prompt changes deserve CI gates like code changes?
options: Because prompts are compiled before deployment
Because prompts and model versions define behavior — a one-line edit can silently break other cases, and without tests nothing catches it before production
Because CI providers require it
They don't — prompts are too subjective to test
questions.explanation: The classic incident: a tweak fixes one complaint and silently drops citations elsewhere; nothing crashes, so nothing alerts. Treating prompts as behavior-defining source code — eval run plus merge-blocking gate on quality drops — prevents exactly this.

questions.prompt: Run-to-run scores on an unchanged system vary between 84% and 86%. How should the gate treat a new PR scoring 85% against an 86% baseline?
options: Block it — any drop is a regression
Pass it — the difference is within the measured noise floor; gate on tolerances, not any-drop
Block it until the developer explains the drop
Re-run until it scores above baseline
questions.explanation: LLM evals are noisy even at temperature 0. Measure the noise floor by running the suite repeatedly against main, then gate on drops beyond it. Any-drop gates block innocent PRs until the team disables the gate entirely.

questions.prompt: Which gate-design practices does the lesson recommend? (Select all that apply.)
options: Diff per-case status — new failures on previously-passing cases beat aggregate movement as a signal
Zero-tolerance floors for deterministic non-negotiables like schema validity and PII rules
Trigger the expensive judged suite only on paths that affect behavior
Run the full judge suite on every README edit for maximum safety
questions.explanation: Per-case diffs catch compensating failures a flat aggregate hides; non-negotiables get hard floors while statistical tolerance is reserved for fuzzy criteria; and path-scoping keeps the gate fast and cheap enough that nobody turns it off — which is what burning judge calls on README edits leads to.

questions.prompt: What role do canary deploys play after the CI gate?
options: They replace CI gates entirely
They catch what the golden set didn't anticipate: a traffic slice runs the new version while online metrics are compared against control, and surfaced failures join the golden set
They're only for infrastructure changes, not prompts
They run the eval suite a second time
questions.explanation: CI tests what you anticipated; production reveals the rest. Canarying a few percent of traffic with per-version metrics (requires per-request version logging — Module 2) gives evidence to promote or roll back, and every canary-caught failure becomes a permanent golden case.

questions.prompt: Assemble the module: golden set + programmatic checks + calibrated judge + tracing. What does each contribute to a CI regression gate?
options: Test data + deterministic assertions + fuzzy-criteria scoring + production feedback into new cases
Four redundant copies of the same signal
Only the judge matters; the rest is overhead
Tracing replaces the need for the other three
questions.explanation: The gate runs the golden set, scores with rules where possible and the judge for the residue, and tracing feeds production failures back into the set — the full flywheel.

questions.prompt: Why does error analysis precede metric selection?
options: Metrics are expensive to compute
Metrics chosen before reading real traces measure what you imagined failing, not what actually fails
Regulators require error analysis first
Error analysis is faster than metrics
questions.explanation: Read 50–100 traces, cluster the failure modes, count them — then the metrics follow from observed reality. The failure list, not the aggregate, drives development.

questions.prompt: A pairwise judge shows the new prompt winning 58% of comparisons — but you ran each pair in only one order. What's the threat?
options: Nothing; 58% is decisive
Position bias can swing pairwise results by double digits — run both orderings and count only consistent wins
Judges can't do pairwise at all
Ties were impossible
questions.explanation: Favoring whichever answer appears first (or last) is systematic, not random — often larger than the effect you're measuring. Order-swapping is non-negotiable.

questions.prompt: Which gate behaviors respect the statistics of LLM evals? (Select all that apply.)
options: Tolerance thresholds derived from measured run-to-run variance
Per-case status diffs, since aggregates hide compensating changes
Zero-tolerance floors only for deterministic non-negotiables
Blocking any PR whose score drops by 0.1%
questions.explanation: Scores wobble even unchanged; gate on drops beyond the noise floor, diff which cases flipped, and reserve hard floors for schema/PII/policy checks. Any-drop gates get disabled by annoyed teams.

questions.prompt: Production traces are flowing into your observability tool but nobody reads them. What does the module call this?
options: Complete observability
Expensive storage — sampled human review is what converts traces into golden cases, checks, and rubric updates
A compliance win
Sufficient, as long as dashboards exist
questions.explanation: The review session — random traces + thumbs-downs + outliers — is the institutionalized 'look at your data' that closes the flywheel. Unread traces improve nothing.

questions.prompt: A canary deploy surfaces a failure the CI gate missed. What's the final step the module prescribes?
options: Disable the canary
Roll back and add the failure to the golden set so the gate never misses it again
Blame the judge
Increase the traffic percentage
questions.explanation: Production catches what golden sets didn't anticipate; feeding each catch back into the set is how the gate compounds — the eval suite grows where reality showed it was blind.
